content.full_text:
WAYNE K. LEMIEUX
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION
200 NORTH WESTLAKE BLVD., SUITE 102
WESTLAKE VILLAGE, CALIFORNIA 91362-3755
805/495-4770
818/448-8128

WAYNE LEMIEUX                                                FAX 805/495-2787
RICHARD HAMLISH
DOROTHY LOU CRISP

                                        COPY

January 2, 1990


Carroll R. Logue, Sr.
Mayor
City of California City
8216 Dogwood
California City, CA  93505

                    POTENTIAL CONFLICTS OF INTEREST

     During recent months, the City has become increasingly involved in
development decisions which are accompanied by potential conflicts
of interest.  The following supplements our prior correspondence
on the subject of conflicts of interest by providing information
as to when Councilmembers may participate in debate as a member of
the public.

                           EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

     When a conflict of interest exists, a councilmember may not
participate in the discussion as a member of the Council.
Councilmembers may not participate in the debate as member of the
public unless the decision in question involves a direct financial
interest of the councilmember.  The materials which follow
illustrate how this rule is applied.

                              ANALYSIS

Introduction

     This analysis deals with potential conflicts of interest which
arise when the City Council considers non-contractual matters.
Non-contractual matters include most policy decisions such as
general plan amendments, zone changes, the establishment of
development fees or the adoption of ordinances.  Non-contractual
matters are governed by the Political Reform Act which is
administered by the Fair Political Practices Commission.

     The most difficult and reoccurring conflict of interest problems
arise in connection with the review of development policy or

2

development projects.  Accordingly, our analysis will present
examples in this area.

Political Reform Act

Government Code Section 87100 states that no public official may
participate in any decision which he knows, or should know, will
have a material financial effect upon the official.  Hence, a
councilmember who owns property within an area affected by a
general plan amendment must refrain from participating in the
decision on the amendment if the amendment could have a material
effect on the value of the property.  Similarly, a councilmember
could not participate in the decision on a zone change affecting
property which the councilmember owns if the zone change could have
a material effect on the value of the property.  Additionally, a
councilmember could not participate in a decision raising fees for
the industry in which the councilmember earns a livelihood unless
the change in fees is immaterial.  It may be possible to conclude
that a general plan amendment will not have a material financial
effect on the councilmember's holdings particularly if the
amendment covers a large area and the councilmember's holdings are
small.  It is less likely that a court would find a lack of
material financial effect in a case of a zone change or in the case
of establishing fees affecting the councilmember's livelihood.

The Political Reform Act includes one broad exception which
deserves mention.  A councilmember is not disqualified from voting
on a matter which could produce a material financial effect upon
the councilmember if the effect on the councilmember is the same
as the effect on the "public generally".  For example,
councilmembers could vote on water rate increases.  However, the
"public generally" exception does not apply simply because more
than one person is affected by the decision.  For example, a
general plan amendment affecting one-third of the City, a zone
amendment affecting several parcels of land, or a fee change
affecting a particular industry would not fall within this
exception.

FPPC Regulations

Although the Political Reform Act will prohibit a councilmember
from acting on a matter as a councilmember, the Act does not
preclude a councilmember from participating in debate as a member
of the public.  However, the Political Reform Act delegates
responsibility for adopting regulations to the Fair Political
Practices Commission (FPPC) and the FPPC has adopted regulations
describing when a councilmember may participate in the debate as
a member of the public.  In this regard, 2 California Code of
Regulations Section 18700.1 states:

"18700.1 Using Official Position to Influence (87100)
(a) With regard to a governmental decision which
is within or before an official's agency or an agency
appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of his
or her agency, the official is attempting to use his or
her official position to influence the decision if, for
the purpose of influencing the decision, the official
contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant
of the agency. Attempts to influence include, but are
not limited to, appearances or contacts by the official
on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer.
(b) Notwithstanding subsection (a) an official is
not attempting to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision of an agency covered
by subsection (a) if the official:
(1) Appears in the same manner as any other
member of the general public before an agency in
the course of its prescribed governmental function
only to represent himself or herself on a matter
which is related to his or her personal interests.
An official's 'personal interests' include, but are
not limited to:
(A) An interest in real property which
is wholly owned by the official or members of
his or her immediate family.
(B) A business entity wholly owned by the
official or members of his or her immediate
family.
(C) A business entity over which the
official exercises sole direction and control,
or over which the official and his or her
spouse jointly exercise sole direction and
control.
(2) Communicates with the general public or
the press.
(3) Negotiates his or her compensation or the
terms and conditions of his or her employment or
contract.
(4) Prepares drawings or submissions of an
architectural, engineering or similar nature to be
used by a client in connection with a proceeding
before any [sic.] the agency. However, this
provision applies only if the official has no other
direct oral or written contact with the agency with
regard to the client's proceeding before the agency
except for necessary contact with agency staff
concerning the processing or evaluation of the
drawings or submissions prepared by the official.
(5) Appears before a design or architectural
review committee or similar body of which he or she
is a member to present drawings or submissions of"

4

an architectural, engineering or similar nature
which the official has prepared for a client if the
following three criteria are met:
(A) The review committee's sole function
is to review architectural or engineering plans
or designs and to make recommendations in that
instance concerning those plans or designs to
a planning commission or other agency;
(B) The ordinance or other provision of
law requires that the review committee include
architects, engineers or persons in related
professions, and the official was appointed to
the body to fulfill this requirement; and
(C) The official is a sole practitioner.
With regard to a governmental decision which
is within or before an agency not covered by subsection
of the official is attempting to use his or her
official position to influence the decision if, for the
purpose of influencing the decision, the official acts
or purports to act on behalf of, or as the representative
of, his or her agency to any member, officer, employee
or consultant of an agency. Such actions include, but
are not limited to the use of official stationery."

In other words, a councilmember may participate in debate as a
member of the public if the debate before the council directly
and immediately affects the decision before the council directly
and immediately affects the decision before the council directly
decision affects the councilmember's property but not if the
councilmember could not participate in a debate over a general plan
amendment which has a material financial effect on a
councilmember's holdings unless the general plan amendment affected
a handful of parcels including the councilmember's parcel. A
councilmember would be able to participate in the debate concerning
a zone change affecting the councilmember's property. The councilmember would not be able to
participate in public debate concerning industry fees.

CONCLUSION

We realize that the foregoing could have the effect of silencing
some councilmembers during important debate. We are compelled to
offer these comments because it is our duty to alert councilmembers

to the law so that violations can be avoided. It is important that
we assist the City in implementing City policies; it is more
important that the implementation not violate any law.

Very truly yours,

Wayne K. Lemieux
WKL/kls

cc: Donald Minnick
William C. Beverly
Jay Sprague
Lottie Walker
Baldev S. Josan

sections.analysis:
Introduction

This analysis deals with potential conflicts of interest which arise when the City Council considers non-contractual matters. Non-contractual matters include most policy decisions such as general plan amendments, zone changes, the establishment of development fees or the adoption of ordinances. Non-contractual matters are governed by the Political Reform Act which is administered by the Fair Political Practices Commission.

The most difficult and reoccurring conflict of interest problems arise in connection with the review of development policy or

development projects. Accordingly, our analysis will present examples in this area.

Political Reform Act

Government Code Section 87100 states that no public official may participate in any decision which he knows, or should know, will have a material financial effect upon the official. Hence, a councilmember who owns property within an area affected by a general plan amendment must refrain from participating in the decision on the amendment if the amendment could have a material effect on the value of the property. Similarly, a councilmember could not participate in the decision on a zone change affecting property which the councilmember owns if the zone change could have a material effect on the value of the property. Additionally, a councilmember could not participate in a decision raising fees for the industry in which the councilmember earns a livelihood unless the change in fees is immaterial. It may be possible to conclude that a general plan amendment will not have a material financial effect on the councilmember's holdings particularly if the amendment covers a large area and the councilmember's holdings are small. It is less likely that a court would find a lack of material financial effect in a case of a zone change or in the case of establishing fees affecting the councilmember's livelihood.

The Political Reform Act includes one broad exception which deserves mention. A councilmember is not disqualified from voting on a matter which could produce a material financial effect upon the councilmember if the effect on the councilmember is the same as the effect on the "public generally". For example, councilmembers could vote on water rate increases. However, the "public generally" exception does not apply simply because more than one person is affected by the decision. For example, a general plan amendment affecting one-third of the City, a zone amendment affecting several parcels of land, or a fee change affecting a particular industry would not fall within this exception.

FPPC Regulations

Although the Political Reform Act will prohibit a councilmember from acting on a matter as a councilmember, the Act does not preclude a councilmember from participating in debate as a member of the public. However, the Political Reform Act delegates responsibility for adopting regulations to the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC) and the FPPC has adopted regulations describing when a councilmember may participate in the debate as a member of the public. In this regard, 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18700.1 states:

"18700.1 Using Official Position to Influence (87100)
(a) With regard to a governmental decision which is within or before an official's agency or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of his or her agency, the official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer.
(b) Notwithstanding subsection (a) an official is not attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision of an agency covered by subsection (a) if the official:
(1) Appears in the same manner as any other member of the general public before an agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function solely to represent himself or herself on a matter which is related to his or her personal interests. An official's 'personal interests' include, but are not limited to:
(A) An interest in real property which is wholly owned by the official or members of his or her immediate family.
(B) A business entity wholly owned by the official or members of his or her immediate family.
(C) A business entity over which the official exercises sole direction and control, or over which the official and his or her spouse jointly exercise sole direction and control.
(2) Communicates with the general public or the press.
(3) Negotiates his or her compensation or the terms and conditions of his or her employment or contract.
(4) Prepares drawings or submissions of an architectural, engineering or similar nature to be used by a client in connection with a proceeding before any [sic.] the agency. However, this provision applies only if the official has no other direct oral or written contact with the agency with regard to the client's proceeding before the agency except for necessary contact with agency staff concerning the processing or evaluation of the drawings or submissions prepared by the official.
(5) Appears before a design or architectural review committee or similar body of which he or she is a member to present drawings or submissions of

an architectural, engineering or similar nature which the official has prepared for a client if the following three criteria are met:
(A) The review committee's sole function is to review architectural or engineering plans or designs and to make recommendations in that instance concerning those plans or designs to a planning commission or other agency;
(B) The ordinance or other provision of law requires that the review committee include architects, engineers or persons in related professions, and the official was appointed to the body to fulfill this requirement; and
(C) The official is a sole practitioner.
(c) With regard to a governmental decision which is within or before an agency not covered by subsection (a), the official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, his or her agency to any member, officer, employee or consultant of an agency. Such actions include, but are not limited to the use of official stationery."

In other words, a councilmember may participate in debate as a member of the public if the decision before the council directly and immediately affects the councilmember's property but not if the decision affects the councilmember indirectly. For example, a councilmember could not participate in a debate over a general plan amendment which has a material financial effect on the councilmember's holdings unless the general plan amendment affected a handful of parcels including the councilmember's parcel. A councilmember would be able to participate in the debate as a member of the public concerning a zone change affecting the councilmember's property. The councilmember would not be able to participate in public debate concerning industry fees.
